story:
The monster’s breath smelled like decayed skunk, hot and sticky enough to make me gag. I clamped a hand over my mouth. My chameleon suit could keep me unseen, but it couldn’t hide puke. Or pee, for that matter—another problem I really didn’t want to test.

I’d picked a massive tree for cover, which wasn’t exactly genius, considering the giant tiger-thing sharpening its claws on the other side. I’d never been good at hiding. I’m not good at much on this planet. I should’ve ordered Alora to fetch water for our hydrogen generator, but no—I’m Captain Williams. Lead by example. My promotion is going to get me killed.

When the creature wandered off, I dared a peek and almost gasped out loud. Elephant-sized, striped like a Bengal, with a black mane and a spiked ball on its tail—intimidating and, I’ll admit, reluctantly cool. Tigerzilla paused, sniffed, and the breeze betrayed me. I ducked back, praying it didn’t add “marooned surveyor” to the menu.

Heavy paws cracked dead limbs as it came around the trunk, its breath turning my stomach inside out. I didn’t pee. Animals are good at smelling things. My suit couldn’t help with that either. I grabbed a rock and tossed it far. Tigerzilla bolted after the sound. I bolted the other way.

Invisibility doesn’t help when something can smell your fear. The beast barreled after me. Screaming seemed like the only productive option. I spotted a hole in a giant tree, big enough for me, not for it. If it was deep enough, I might live. I dove in, wedging myself against the wood as its forearm swept in, claws scraping. It came up short. I exhaled. All I had to do was wait.

Waiting turned into a stalemate with only one of us able to move. It gave up pawing and flopped down outside like a cat guarding a mouse hole. I shouted. I begged. I lost my voice and curled up, imagining every kind of death that ends in “-ation.”

Then I heard Alora. I tried to warn her, but Tigerzilla leapt up and charged toward the sound. I scrambled out, ready to watch her get eaten—and found her petting it like a friendly dog.

“What is going on?” I croaked.

Alora told it to sit. It sat.

“I heard a little girl screaming for help,” she said. Unless there was a child wedged in my hole, she meant me.

“For the record,” I said, “my scream is considered manly.”

She arched an eyebrow. “Was it about this guy?”

“He was about to eat me!”

“He was playing. I’ve seen dozens since we crashed yesterday.”

Dozens? On what kind of planet? She dismissed the creature, and it trotted off, spikes and all.

We had to hurry. If the hydrogen generator ran dry, our chameleon shield could fail, and advertising a downed ship on a strange world sounded like a terrible idea. Alora’s confidence made leadership…complicated. Most androids I’d worked with were barely competent. She wasn’t most androids.

“You’re unique,” I admitted.

“That’s because I wasn’t built on Earth.”

“Where then?”

“Outside the Union.”

That was all she gave me.

We neared our star cruiser. I couldn’t quite remember where we’d parked—crashed. Alora pointed at a grain field. “Over there.”

“Cool. That’s what I thought.”

She shook her head. “You’re unbelievable.”

Before heading back, she led me to the spring I’d been trying to reach in the first place, and together we filled the water bladders from a clear trickle running through the roots. With the generator topped off and the shield safe, we could hunt for dylanium tomorrow. Tonight we’d rest. And if I was lucky, I’d annoy Alora a little—purely for morale.
